coredata.dc:title: Adaptive and safe fast charging of lithium-ion batteries via hybrid model learning and control barrier functions
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Hailemichael, Habtamu
Ayalew, Beshah
coredata.dc:description: Prolonged charging times remain a critical challenge for Lithium-ion batteries (LiBs), which are the leading energy storage solution for many applications. This work proposes a framework for generating a safe and optimal charging policy by leveraging in-situ operational data streams from LiBs. The operational data is used to continuously learn a virtual hybrid model decomposed into physics-based and neural residual components in a manner that maintains maximum explainability. Then the fast-charging policy is learned via deep reinforcement learning (DRL) with latent imagination using the hybrid model. Since the DRL policy is based on a continually adapted hybrid model, the resulting charging profile is expected to be optimal for the prevailing LiB age and operational conditions. By conducting DRL exploration on the virtual hybrid model, the framework mitigates safety risks and operational inconveniences associated with on-battery DRL training. Additionally, the framework incorporates formal safety guarantees using a control barrier function (CBF) filter that includes state of charge (SOC), voltage and temperature safety and operational constraints. The DRL policy is trained to promote rapid SOC increases while penalizing CBF constraint violations. After training the DRL+CBF policy on the hybrid model, the fast charging policy is implemented on the physical LiB. Both experimental and simulation results demonstrate that the framework delivers adaptive charging policies tailored to various operational conditions, such as ambient and battery temperature, while adhering to safety constraints. In experimental tests, the proposed framework delivers up to 8X faster charging compared to the manufacturer-recommended constant current constant voltage (CCCV) profile.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: Carolina Consortium: Core Hybrid journals RAP 2025
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion batteries
Fast charging
Hybrid models
Reinforcement learning
Control barrier functions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: DRL-based fast charging policy generation and CBF formulation
Online implementation of DRL fast charging policy
Implementation of action-bound aware CBF on the virtual hybrid model
Experimental implementation of DRL fast charging with CBF filter

Approved for public release; distribution is unlimited. OPSEC9341

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Framework for data-driven adaptive safe fast charging policy training with DRL and CBF filter.

Evaluation of the virtual hybrid model in tracking the terminal voltage and LiB temperature outputs of three experiments with different temperature and SOC initializations. The plot for CCCV charging in Experiment 2 is truncated for clarity.

Illustration of the impact of starting action-bound CBF constraints at different temperature thresholds. Additionally, this results underscore the importance of incorporating DRL policies instead of relying solely on the maximum allowable current provided by the CBF filter.
Experimental set up to execute DRL fast charging policy.
Experimental schematic for real-time implementation of DRL+CBF fast charging policy.
Experimental DRL fast charging implementation from different initial temperatures.
Experimental comparison of the proposed DRL+CBF fast charging approach with the manufacturer recommended CCCV profile.
Current, voltage, and temperature measurements from an LiB cell used to train the virtual hybrid model. The charging profiles are truncated for clarity.

head.ce:title: Adaptive and safe fast charging of lithium-ion batteries via hybrid model learning and control barrier functions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Automotive Engineering, Clemson University, Greenville, 29607, SC, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Automotive Engineering, Clemson University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: organization=Department of Automotive Engineering, Clemson University, city=Greenville, postcode=29607, state=SC, country=USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Prolonged charging times remain a critical challenge for Lithium-ion batteries (LiBs), which are the leading energy storage solution for many applications. This work proposes a framework for generating a safe and optimal charging policy by leveraging in-situ operational data streams from LiBs. The operational data is used to continuously learn a virtual hybrid model decomposed into physics-based and neural residual components in a manner that maintains maximum explainability. Then the fast-charging policy is learned via deep reinforcement learning (DRL) with latent imagination using the hybrid model. Since the DRL policy is based on a continually adapted hybrid model, the resulting charging profile is expected to be optimal for the prevailing LiB age and operational conditions. By conducting DRL exploration on the virtual hybrid model, the framework mitigates safety risks and operational inconveniences associated with on-battery DRL training. Additionally, the framework incorporates formal safety guarantees using a control barrier function (CBF) filter that includes state of charge (SOC), voltage and temperature safety and operational constraints. The DRL policy is trained to promote rapid SOC increases while penalizing CBF constraint violations. After training the DRL+CBF policy on the hybrid model, the fast charging policy is implemented on the physical LiB. Both experimental and simulation results demonstrate that the framework delivers adaptive charging policies tailored to various operational conditions, such as ambient and battery temperature, while adhering to safety constraints. In experimental tests, the proposed framework delivers up to 8X faster charging compared to the manufacturer-recommended constant current constant voltage (CCCV) profile.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:def-description.ce:para: Parameters of the observer neural network
Parameters of the physics neural network
Final time of the simulation horizon [s]
Parameters of the residual neural network

ce:section.ce:para: Extended charging time for lithium-ion batteries (LiBs) remains a major challenge to the widespread adoption of electric vehicles (EVs), diminishing their competitive edge compared to fossil fuel-powered vehicles. Fast charging of LiBs entails using high C-rates to accelerate charging. However this must be done while preventing the onset of degradation that is associated with high C-rate charging, including lithium plating, the rapid growth of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) film, and structural degradation [1,2]. The common approach to minimize these risks across varying battery age and operational conditions is to set conservative charge profiles (i.e., moderate C-rates that cover the most conditions). An alternative to these cautious strategies involves relaxing the conservativeness by adapting the charging profiles to the battery age and use conditions using operational LiB data. To that end, this paper introduces a novel framework that adapts charging profiles through Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) using data typically available from Battery Management Systems (BMS). The framework ensures safety by avoiding on-battery DRL exploration while training and integrating a formal Control Barrier Function (CBF) safety filter.

Commonly used charging strategies typically employ heuristic protocols such as Constant Current Constant Voltage (CCCV), Constant Power Constant Voltage (CPCV), Pulse Charging, and their variations. The CCCV and CPCV methods generally begin with a higher current (or power) and maintain a constant voltage in the later phases. The specifications for the initial current/power in CCCV/CPCV, as well as the pulse parameters in pulse charging, are empirically selected conservatively to ensure safe operation across various battery environments and aging conditions. Generating these heuristic profiles requires extensive lifetime experiments on lithium-ion batteries (LiBs) under various operational conditions and charging strategies. Even with machine learning helping to expedite some of these experiments [3], obtaining a comprehensive heuristic still demands significant effort.

Model-based methods offer partial customizability by leveraging LiB Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) models to optimize charging schedules. These methods often integrate Equivalent Circuit Models (ECMs) coupled with thermal and aging models. Several studies have explored this area, including [4–6], which apply Model Predictive Control (MPC) techniques using ECM models to determine LiB charging profiles. Other methods using ECMs include dynamic programming [7,8], Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR) [9], genetic algorithms [10], and control barrier functions [11]. However, the accuracy of model-based approaches hinges on how well the models capture the dynamic behavior of LiBs across varying loads, operational conditions, and aging stages. ECM models, while widely used, are overly simplistic and struggle to capture the complex electrochemical and thermal dynamics of LiBs accurately. Consequently, charging profiles derived from ECMs inherit these limitations. Electrochemical models, such as the Pseudo-Two-Dimensional Model (P2D) [12] and its simplified Single Particle (SP) variant [13], address these shortcomings by modeling the electrochemical processes of LiBs more closely. Some studies employ reduced-order models (ROM) and SP models for scheduling charging with methods like MPC [14,15], but these simplified versions are less accurate at high C-rates and involve numerous parameters that are challenging to identify, especially considering the evolving nature of these parameters over the LiB’s lifetime.

Other groups of studies have also explored data-driven approaches employing machine learning to devise fast charging strategies based on operational data streams of current, voltage, and temperature. Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) are highly effective in capturing complex nonlinear dynamics inherent in LiBs due to their high expressivity and have been shown to achieve optimal charging policies with methods like DRL [16–18]. Generally, purely data-driven methods lack robustness outside the training data domain. They also fall short in terms of explainability and the crucial insights provided by physics-based models, such as State of Health (SOH) parameters. DRL methods, in particular, require intensive exploration, which, when applied to physical batteries, can introduce safety risks. Even with approaches that focus on learning and adapting the safety region within which the DRL explores – such as using Gaussian Process Regression [19] – on battery exploration can still cause inconveniences for users, especially during the early phases when the DRL policies are not yet fully refined.

To alleviate these shortcomings, in our earlier work [20], we proposed a framework in which we continually train a virtual hybrid model of the LiB and subsequently use that to train DRL charging model. The virtual hybrid model is designed to optimally integrate a first-order RC (Resistor–Capacitor) ECM physics model, which captures the majority of the dynamics for maximum generalizability and explainability, with residual neural network components trained to handle the remaining complexities [21]. In our scheme, the latest version of the virtual hybrid model is utilized to generate the requisite data for training DRL policies via latent imagination of charging profiles across various initial states of the LiB and ambient conditions. Latent imagination refers to an agent’s ability to simulate or predict the consequences of its actions in a learned latent space, without directly interacting with the real environment [22]. This allows the agent to “imagine” future states based on its learned model of the environment, enabling more efficient exploration and decision-making. This approach of exploring DRL on the virtual hybrid model effectively mitigates safety risks and operational disruptions that are typically associated with on-battery exploration during DRL training. Additionally, since the hybrid model is trained to capture LiB dynamics within the prevailing operational environment, the charging policy generated using this model is also optimal for those conditions and is shown to significantly enhance charging speed in simulated experiments.

Our previous method [20] ensured risk-free data generation for DRL training, but relied on severe penalties for voltage and temperature violations to enforce safety, discouraging trajectories that could lead to these violations. However, without utilizing large data (that include all feasible trajectories for the system) and considering systems that drift over time, this entirely data-driven approach to safety enforcement lacks complete certainty in avoiding all unsafe trajectories, highlighting the need for theoretical assurances. Moreover, using harsh penalties seems to force the DRL policies to operate far from trajectories near safety limits, which, in the context of LiB fast charging, are often the most optimal paths. Exploiting these paths requires a disciplined approach to guaranteeing safety.

One of the prominent approaches to integrating formal safety guarantee into RL training and inference involves a hierarchical approach in which the task-oriented RL controller is optimized independently, while separate safety filters are used to evaluate and screen out unsafe actions proposed by the RL controller [23–25]. To keep the system within the safe set, the safety filters must predict the proposed actions’ safety consequences before designating a state–action pair as safe or unsafe. Prior external knowledge in the form of offline data or the dynamic model of the system can be utilized to design these safety filters [23]. In this study, we utilize a Control Barrier Function (CBF) filter [26], a dynamic model-based filter renowned for its excellent scalability and minimal computational requirements [27], to incorporate formal safety guarantees into our hybrid model + DRL framework. CBF ensures safety by ensuring that the system remains within an invariant safe-set defined by a superlevel set of a continuously differentiable function
h
( x )
:
R
n
→ R
. The actions selected by the task-oriented DRL policy are projected into the safe set in such a manner that the proposed actions are modified minimally, and no unsafe actions are passed to the controlled system. In addition to providing formal safety guarantees, by integrating DRL with the CBF filter, we aim to equip the DRL policy with a deeper understanding of the mechanisms that lead to safety violations, allowing it to navigate near safety boundaries without the need for overly harsh penalties.

As mentioned before, the CBF filter must accurately predict the immediate and longer-term horizon safety implications of proposed actions using the dynamic model of the LiB. Recent work of [11] formulates SOC, voltage and temperature CBF constraints for LiB. The work uses a cascading approach where quadratic programming (QP) is employed to optimize the charging current with respect to SOC and voltage CBF constraints, followed by non-QP optimization with temperature CBF constraint. However, due to the non-affine nature of the temperature CBF constraint (involving quadratic terms), certain trajectories may lead to states where CBF projections fail to provide viable solutions within the charging current bounds. In addition, the above work directly employed CBFs to generate charging policies in which the maximum permissible charging rate determined by the CBF filter is applied to charge the LiB. However, this approach does not consistently yield an optimal charging policy as the maximum CBF current magnitude does not always align with optimal charging trajectories. In this paper, we develop a CBF filter using the learned virtual hybrid model that guarantees safety within the charger’s current bound and integrates it with a DRL-based task controller to achieve a robust optimal charging policy. Moreover, having a dominant physics component in the hybrid model and a physics fitting that considers prior knowledge on the bound of the physics parameters enables the CBF to be explainable and verifiable. In addition, since the hybrid model is continuously updated using the data generated from the field, we will have a continuously evolving CBF filter rather than a static and conservative one.
The main contributions of this work can be summarized as follows:

ce:list-item.ce:para: A framework is proposed where a virtual hybrid model of the LiB is learned continually and utilized to determine an adaptive charging policy using DRL.

A CBF safety filter is integrated with DRL to enforce safety with SOC, voltage, and temperature constraints, and including charger’s current bounds. The CBF filter utilizes the learned hybrid model to continually adapt to use conditions and ageing.
The study includes various demonstrations to showcase the effectiveness of the proposed adaptive fast-charging framework through both real-time implementations and simulated experiments on a LiB.

ce:section.ce:para: The rest of the paper is organized as follows. Section 2 outlines the problem formulation, providing a brief overview of continual hybrid model learning and discussing how to leverage the hybrid model to train the DRL model. This section also covers the design of action-bound aware CBF filter for LiBs and their integration with the DRL policy. Section 3 discusses the training setup and performance of the hybrid model, followed by an analysis of the influence of the action-bound CBF constraint. We then present the experimental setup and evaluate the performance of the DRL+CBF policy on a battery cell. Finally, Section 4 concludes the paper.

Fig. 1 illustrates the schematic of the proposed framework. Operational data from LiBs, including current, voltage, and LiB temperature {i,v,T}, are continuously collected and utilized for continual learning of the virtual hybrid model. The virtual hybrid model is subsequently used to simulate various charging profiles starting in different LiB initial states and ambient conditions through latent imagination. The resulting state trajectory data is then employed to train the DRL fast charging policy, treating LiB charging as a Markov Decision Process (MDP). CBFs are integrated to filter unsafe actions both during data generation in the virtual model and in the implementation of learned charging policies on the actual LiB. We will briefly discuss the hybrid model formulation and training approach and subsequently discuss the details of the DRL training set up for optimal charging with a CBF filter.

In this section, we offer a brief overview of our hybrid model training approach. More detailed discussions can be found in [21]. Following the notation used in [28], we consider a partially observed system characterized by state variables
Z
t
, model input
U
t
, and output
Y

at time t . Here,

denotes an m -dimensional vector,
Z

]
. Similarly, U and Y denote trajectories of the model input and output, respectively. The function f captures the dynamics governing the state trajectory, while g represents the output function.

Our goal is to capture the trajectory of the state Z with an estimate X . This entails approximating the true dynamics f with a function h to explain the measurements of the observed components Y . The function h results in a trajectory in the available normed vector space
( H , ‖ ⋅ ‖ )

. We combine contributions of an adopted physics-based model
h

resulting in a hybrid model given as follows:

In systems like LiBs, the parameters present in the physics model are nonlinear functions of the system state (e.g. impedances are functions of SOC and temperature). In addition, having latent states (like SOC), the model fitting need to also consider recognizing initial states. Accordingly, assuming knowledge of the available physics model trajectories
(
H
p
)
, the learning objective is then to optimize the residual neural network parameterized by w , the nonlinear relations of the physics model parameters with system states
θ

However, solving for all optimization parameters in (3) in a stable manner is challenging for complex systems such as LiBs. Therefore, we pursue a sequential approach that allows us to optimize each variable separately. Starting with the assumption that the initial state is known and the physics model is structurally complete (encompassing all necessary terms to accurately represent the dynamics) [29], the approach first optimizes for the physics model parameters as if the whole dynamics were expressed with just the physics model. Subsequently, the residual component is minimized by training a hybrid model incorporating the refined physics model.

Therefore, we adopt a sequential approach that enables the separate optimization of each component. Starting with the assumption that the initial state is known and the physics model is structurally complete – i.e., it includes all necessary terms to accurately capture the system dynamics [29] – the approach first optimizes the parameters of the physics model, treating it as if it fully represents the system. Subsequently, the residual dynamics are captured by training a hybrid model that incorporates the refined physics model, thereby minimizing the remaining discrepancy.

To model LiBs in the format (2), we combine a first-order resistive-capacitive (RC) equivalent circuit model (ECM) with a lumped thermal model, forming the physics component of our hybrid model described in (5). At any time t , the system’s states include the SOC, the current associated with diffusion voltage across resistor R (referred to as the diffusion current in this work), and the temperature, denoted as

As mentioned before, the parameters of the ECM model are significantly dependent on the states of the battery. To learn these dependencies, we employ two neural networks. The first neural network aims to model the influence of temperature on the battery’s capacity, denoted as
Q
ϕ
( T )
. Another neural network is utilized to represent how the parameters
R
0
, C
, and R are influenced by temperature, SOC, and the direction of current flow, indicated by

[31]. We assume that the Open Circuit Voltage (OCV)-SOC curve is known, and the model training does not extend to identifying thermal parameters such as

Dynamics not captured by the physics model are addressed by the residual network, which comprises two types of residuals. The first one makes minimal adjustments to the physics model voltage output with the residuals of
{

. Since the same corrections to the voltage can be attributed to different combination of these residuals, we include a weighting term B in the loss function to promote a well-defined optimization problem with good numerical stability.

So far, training for both the physics and residual components assumes that the initial SOC z and latent diffusion current
i
r
states are known beforehand. However, in practical scenarios where only terminal voltage v and temperature T measurements are available, we propose implementing a neural observer [32] for initial state recognition (state estimation). The neural observer network, parameterized with β , uses feedback from measurements to adjust initial assumptions. Specifically, using the error between the measured and the hybrid model terminal voltage output
E
t − 1
, the neural observer estimates z and
i
r
. The dynamics of the observer state can be characterized as follows:

are the neural observer corrections for the SOC and diffusion current. To ensure stable convergence the neural observer is trained with a Lyapunov loss function defined as follows:
(14)

for a constant
γ > 0
[33].

The initial state
X
t
c
for training the physics and residual models is determined by propagating the observer forward until a predefined time
t
c
. This creates a mutual dependence between the hybrid model and the neural observer: the physics and residual components of the hybrid model depend on the observer to provide an accurate initial state, while the observer benefits from the hybrid model for accurate estimation of latent states. To tackle this interdependence, we propose employing a concurrent gradient descent (GD) algorithm for the joint training of the physics, residual, and neural observer models. This approach iteratively updates all neural networks simultaneously, as outlined in Algorithm 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DRL-based fast charging policy generation and CBF formulation
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, we outline the DRL formulation used to train the fast charging policy using the virtual hybrid model. Additionally, we detail the formulation of the CBF filter and its integration with the DRL framework.

We formulate the LiB charging as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) with states s , actions a , a reward function
r
( s , a )
, and discount factor γ . The DRL state comprises of SOC
( z )
, diffusion current, terminal voltage, LiB temperature, and ambient temperature, i.e.,
s =
{ z ,
i
r
, v , T ,
T
a m b
}
encompassing both measurable and latent states available in the virtual model. The charging current magnitude is the action
a =
i
R L
. The DRL framework adopts an actor–critic architecture, where the actor proposes the control action and the critic evaluates the action’s effectiveness given the environment’s state. The actor-network outputs mean and variance values, from which different charging current magnitudes are explored for a given state by sampling from a normal distribution. DRL training data is generated by running exploration policies through the most recently learned virtual hybrid model based on prevailing LiB states.

The proposed current action is filtered/projected by CBF filter to
i
f
and applied to the virtual hybrid model as depicted in Fig. 1. At each time step, a reward is assigned based on the resulting state. We structured the reward primarily to promote higher SOC increase
( Δ z
). However, if the proposed action violates the CBF limit, we impose a small penalty to encourage the DRL to acknowledge the presence of the CBF filter before applying the proposed current. As a result, when an action is projected by the CBF filter, the DRL algorithm can differentiate between the original action and its projected counterpart.
(16)
r = Δ z +
r
C B F
where
r
C B F
=
w
C B F
⋅
(
i
R L
−
i
f
)
where
w
C B F
represents a weight used to adjust the impact of the penalty for violating the CBF. The triplet comprising state, DRL proposed action (
i
R L
), and reward is stored in a memory buffer for subsequent training. In this study, we employed Maximum A Posteriori Policy Optimization (MPO) for DRL training; however, other state-of-the-art deep DRL algorithms are also applicable for this task. For detailed implementation specifics of MPO, we direct interested readers to [34].

The safety and optimality of the DRL+CBF policy depend on how accurately the hybrid model captures the current dynamics of the LiB. To ensure this alignment, the most recent version of the hybrid model – trained under prevailing conditions – should be used during the DRL+CBF training process. However, while frequent updates to the hybrid model can mitigate model drift, they also incur large computational overhead. Therefore, the update frequency of both the hybrid model and the associated DRL+CBF policy must balance the trade-off between the safety and optimality of the DRL+ CBF model and the associated computational cost. In general, extreme operating conditions (such as extreme temperature and/or high C-rate operations) necessitate more frequent updates to the hybrid model to capture accelerated ageing effects.

Consider a nonlinear control system:
(17)
x
̇
= f
x , u
where f is locally Lipschitz,
x ∈
R
n
is the state of the system,
u ∈
R
m
is the control input. Assume a safe set defined by
C =
x ∈
R
n
| h
x
≥ 0
, where
h :
R
n
→ R
is a continuously differentiable function. Then h is a CBF if there exists an extended class
K
∞
function α such that for all
x ∈ I n t
C
=
x ∈
R
n
: h
x
> 0
:
(18)
sup
u ∈ U
∂ f
( x , u )
∂ u
≥ − α
h
( x )

where U is the feasible set for the control action. Typically, CBF filtering involves projection, an optimization process where the objective function measures the normed distance between the proposed action and the closest safe action, subject to the constraints imposed by the CBF constraints. For a proposed control action
u
p
,
(19)
u
∗
=
arg min
u ∈ U
1
2
‖
u
p
− u ‖
2
s.t.
∂ f
( x , u )
∂ u
≥ − α
h
( x )

When these CBF constraints are linear with respect of the action u , as in the case of affine nonlinear control system
x
̇
= f
( x )
+ g
( x )
u
, with locally Lipschitz f and g , this optimization problem reduces to a quadratic program (QP) in the filtered action u:
(20)
u
∗
=
arg min
u ∈ U
1
2
‖
u
p
− u ‖
2
s.t.
L
f
h
( x )
+
L
g
h
( x )
u ≥ − α
( h
( x )
)
where
L
f
,
L
g
are Lie derivative operators [35].

The CBF constraints define crucial safety and operational limits of charging LiBs. The CBF constraint for the SOC prevents overcharging, ensuring the SOC remains within safe boundaries. One of the most common degradation mechanisms, lithium plating, occurs when the anode overpotential drops below safe levels [2]. Given the absence of anode overpotential in the hybrid model state, we implement a stringent terminal voltage CBF constraint to mitigate the risk of lithium plating [16]. Additionally, a CBF is utilized to prevent thermal runaway and temperature related degradations including the expansion of the SEI layer.

The SOC CBF constraint is straightforward as the SOC dynamics is of relative degree one [26].
(21)
z
̇
=
i
Q
+
z
r
≥ −
α
z
z
m a x
− z
where
α
z
is an extended class
K
∞
function for SOC constraint, and
z
m a x
is the maximum allowable SOC. The terminal voltage is of mixed relative degrees [36] with respect to the current, as the current affects the
R
0
i
term in zero degrees and
R
i
R
and
v
O C V
terms in the first degree. With the assumption that the
R
i
R
and
v
O C V
terms change slowly (which is generally true in practice), we will only consider the zero-degree component, and the terminal voltage CBF constraint becomes:
(22)
−
v
o c v
+ R
i
r
+
R
0
i −
v
r
≥
−
v
m a x
where
v
m a x
is the maximum allowable terminal voltage. The temperature is modeled via a lumped thermal model, and unlike the SOC and voltage constraints, it results in a nonaffine CBF constraint including a square current term:
(23)
− A h
T −
T

T

− T )
where
α
T
is an extended class
K
∞
function for temperature constraint. One could simplify this by lumping the square current term
w =
i
2
to make the temperature CBF constraint linear with respect to w .
(24)
− A h
T −
T

T

− T )

However, even with this adjustment, the CBF filter will fail in situations where the
w
R
0
term must be negative to fulfill the CBF constraint (
i
r
2
R
term drives the thermal dynamics, which die down slowly even with zero applied current). To ensure that the CBF can enforce constraints within the action limits despite the thermal dynamics driven by the diffusion current, we propose to require that the heat generated by the diffusion current and the residual term is balanced by the heat dissipated through convection. This balance is expressed as
B = A h
T −
T
a m b
−

r
2
R −
C
e f f
T
r
≥ 0
, ensuring no additional temperature increase occurs when current is not applied. The CBF form of this constraint, referred to as the action-bound constraint, is given as
B
̇
> −

b
( B )

(25)

− A h

where

b
is an extended class
K
∞
function for the action-bound constraint. Being quadratic constraint in the control action i , this results in Quadratically Constrained Quadratic Program (QCQP) for the CBF projection.

ce:para.ce:italic: Remark: In practice, mandating that heat generated by the diffusion current must be offset by the heat dissipated through convection can lead to overly conservative charging profiles. This is attributed to the fact that the condition necessitates states far from maximum temperature to have lower diffusion currents, thereby enforcing smaller applied currents as a consequence. As a result, we propose applying such an action-bound CBF constraint when the LiB approaches within a predetermined threshold of its maximum temperature limit (i.e

ce:section.ce:para:
With the CBF constraints in place, the charging action is filtered to the nearest safe magnitude. To maintain numerical stability, we adopt a cascading approach similar to [11] where we first apply the SOC and terminal voltage CBF constraints to get
i
v s o c
∗
.
(26)
i
v s o c
∗

i

Subsequently, we further filter
i

∗
using the temperature and action-bound CBF constraints to get the filtered

f

as shown in (27). The additional
0 ≤

≤

constraint in (27) makes sure that the QCQP has a unique solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Online implementation of DRL fast charging policy
ce:section.ce:para:
After training the DRL fast charging policy, it is applied to the actual LiB for online implementation. However, this requires the use of an observer to estimate latent states such as SOC
( z )
and diffusion current
(
i
r
)
online. To achieve this, we utilize an Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) [37] that incorporates ECM parameters obtained from the physics-based component of the latest hybrid model. Since the UKF is a closed-loop estimator, the residual component is not mandatory. The state space model used for the UKF is as follows:
(28)
z
t
̇
i
̇
r , t
=
0
−
1
τ
(
z
t
,
T
t
,
i
s g n , t
)
z
t
i
r , t
+
1
Q
t
(
T
t
)
1
τ
(
z
t
,
T
t
,
i
s g n , t
)
i
t
(29)
V
t
=
O C V
(
z
t
)
− R
(
z
t
,
T
t
,
i
s g n , t
)
i
r , t
−
R
0
(
z
t
,
T
t
,
i
s g n , t
)
i
t

The framework illustrated in Fig. 1 enables the continuous learning of the hybrid model (end-to-end) from data streams, ensuring that the DRL uses the most up-to-date model. Model adaptations can be periodical or event-triggered. In this section, we demonstrate the functionality of the various components of the pipeline using sample data collected from LiB.

We trained the hybrid model by collecting current, voltage, and temperature data
{ v , i , T }
from a 5Ah LG-M50 high-energy 21700 cylindrical cell. We collect the training dataset in a manner that simulates an actual operation of an LiB cell in an EV. Accordingly, we utilized an Arbin BT2000 to collect
{ v , i }
data, employing actual driving profiles from a BMW i3 [38] scaled appropriately for cell-level testing. A total of 42 such driving profiles with a duration of 20 min each are utilized with a sampling rate of 1 s. Fig. A.8 in the appendix illustrates the measured data. Additional 8 constant current discharge and 12 CCCV charging profiles are also collected initialized in different C-rates. Although the data used for training the hybrid model is not extensive, the model still captures the system dynamics well, as will be shown. We used a thermal chamber to initialize the ambient temperature from different levels (
0
∘
C– 45
∘
C
), and the battery temperature is measured with a J-type thermocouple (4YV56) placed on the surface of the LiB cell.
Fig. 2 shows the performance of the fully trained hybrid model with evaluations including driving cycles in different ambient temperatures and charging with CCCV profile.
• Experiment 1: Drive cycle profile (for 20 min) at low SOC and high LiB and ambient temperatures
• Experiment 2: CCCV charging at high LiB and ambient temperatures
• Experiment 3: Drive cycle profile (for 20 min) at high SOC and low LiB and ambient temperatures

Since the SOC state is latent, we use the neural observer state recognition (along with the residual and physics components) for the initial
T
c
= 200
s to bring the model to the actual SOC and diffusion current (initial state recognition) as shown in Fig. 2(a). After
T
c
, the neural observer is switched off, and only the hybrid model (physics and residual) predicts the state evolution of the LiB. As can be seen from Fig. 2(b), the hybrid model effectively captures the terminal voltage and temperature of the LiB during the application of driving profiles and CCCV charging, across various initializations of temperature and SOC. The RMSE error for voltage and temperature is
26 mV
and
0 . 4
∘
C
, respectively, which we deem sufficient accuracy for subsequent utilization of the hybrid model. As the hybrid model decomposition designed to encourage maximal model representation with the physics component, the resulting minimal residuals are shown in Fig. 2(c) for the SOC, diffusion current, voltage, and temperature. The identified physics parameters are shown in Fig. 2(d), in which one can also see the adaptation of such parameters with battery states (temperature and SOC). We encourage readers to refer to our previous work [21] for a more detailed discussion on how explainability is maintained through disciplined decomposition, as well as ablation studies highlighting the importance of the different components of the hybrid model (ECM physics model, neural residual, and neural observer state recognition).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Implementation of action-bound aware CBF on the virtual hybrid model
ce:section.ce:para: In this subsection, we will demonstrate the importance of utilizing action-bound aware CBF filter and how to leverage DRL to achieve the optimal charging trajectory while adhering to these CBF constraints. As mentioned previously, the nonaffine nature of the temperature CBF constraint necessitates the inclusion of an additional action-bound CBF constraint. Without this consideration, certain charging trajectories may lead to situations where the CBF filter cannot effectively enforce the maximum temperature constraint. One may observe that violations of the temperature CBF constraint are more likely to occur when the diffusion current is high or in cells within tightly packed modules, where limited convection heat dissipation is due to the combination of small cooling areas, low convective heat transfer coefficients, and high ambient temperatures. As proposed in the previous section, such scenarios can be accommodated via additional CBF constraints that require the heat generation through the diffusion current term (and the residual term) to be matched by the heat rejected out of the LiB when the charging current is zero.

To demonstrate the importance of the action-bound aware CBF, we employ high diffusion current initialization under a relatively high ambient temperature (
35
∘
C
) simulated in the trained hybrid model of a single LiB cell. The results are shown in Fig. 3. To demonstrate the performance of the different versions of the CBF filter, we assumed the maximum current limit (3C) is proposed to charge the LiB. When applying this maximum current filtered by CBF with constraints on SOC, voltage, and temperature (without including the action-bound CBF constraint), we observe that these CBF constraints alone cannot always effectively mitigate the temperature increase induced by the diffusion current. This leads to the temperature exceeding the allowable maximum of
45
∘
C
, as shown in “CBF without action-bound”, even when zero charging current is applied for an extended duration. It is also important to note that the hybrid model is for a single cell where all cell surfaces are exposed to ambient. In practical scenarios, cells are typically packaged within modules (packs), which facilitate faster temperature rise and, as a result, significantly increases the likelihood of violation of the thermal constraint.

To reduce the conservativeness of the resulting charging trajectory, we propose applying the action-bound CBF when the temperature gets close to the maximum allowable temperature. Accordingly, “Action-bound CBF at
T
threshold
=
T
max
− 5
” in Fig. 3 refers to incorporating the action-bound CBF constraint (in addition to the other CBF constraints) when the LiB crosses the temperature thresholds of
T
max
− 5
. This CBF results in preemptively reducing the current before nearing the maximum temperature, thereby mitigating the diffusion current. This ensures that when the temperature approaches
T
max
, the temperature CBF can enforce the temperature limit effectively, potentially by applying zero charging current. We can further explore the impact of applying action-bound CBFs at different temperature thresholds. “Action-bound CBF at
T
threshold
=
T
max
− 8
” illustrates the effect of activating the action-bound CBF in the same experiment earlier, specifically at
T
max
− 8
. This CBF makes the charging profile more conservative, as evidenced by the slower increase in SOC compared to initiating at
T
threshold
=
T
max
− 5
. Pushing down the temperature threshold to activate the action-bound CBF even earlier (or even using the action-bound CBF for all temperatures) exacerbates the conservativeness of the resulting charging profile. On the other hand, using late activation of the action-bound CBF does not provide adequate time to effectively mitigate thermal inertia effects. This often leads to violations of the maximum temperature constraint, as demonstrated by the “Action-bound CBF at
T
threshold
=
T
max
− 2
” initialized at
T
max
− 2
. This highlights the importance of selecting an appropriate initialization temperature for action-bound CBF that achieves a balance between performance optimization and constraint satisfaction. However, in general, under conditions of high
T
max
, limited cooling capacity, and high ambient temperatures – where tighter thermal control is typically required – a conservative
T
threshold
with large back off should be used.

Simply applying the maximum CBF allowable current, like the cases so far, does not always ensure an optimal charging profile. The presence of temperature and action-bound CBFs can create dead zones where no charging current is applied. Due to the highly nonlinear temperature dynamics in LiBs, the maximum CBF allowable current does not necessarily result in the optimal trajectory. Fig. 3 also compares the CBF-generated profiles with the current profile of DRL+CBF policy trained according to Section 2.2, incorporating the action-bound CBF starting at
T
max
− 5
. The DRL policy takes into account the nonlinear dynamics of temperature and voltage, as well as the constraints imposed by CBF, to formulate a charging strategy. Accordingly, as depicted in the figure, the “DRL + CBF with action-bound CBF at
T
threshold
=
T
max
− 5
” achieves faster charging compared to all scenarios where the maximum allowable current with CBF filter is considered.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental implementation of DRL fast charging with CBF filter
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 depicts our experimental set up for implementing the proposed DRL+CBF fast charging scheme. The DRL inference is executed in a dSPACE Scalexio Autobox unit, where the control current output from the DRL policy is converted to an analog control signal and sent to a DC power supply. The battery cell is placed in a thermal chamber to facilitate experiments under various ambient temperatures, with the LiB temperature monitored using a J-type thermocouple placed on the surface of the LiB. The LiB’s terminal voltage and other sensor voltages are also directly measured. Current measurement is conducted using a current shunt. Both the thermocouple and shunt voltage signals undergo filtering and amplification via signal conditioners.

The dSPACE unit runs the Simulink model version of the DRL charging framework depicted in Fig. 5, which comprises various components of the framework organized into different blocks. The input processing block includes tasks such as calculating the current from shunt voltage measurements and transforming the inputs from thermocouples. The SOC and diffusion current states are computed using the UKF block, which derives the physics parameters
{ Q , R ,
R
0
, C }
from the hybrid model block to implement the observer dynamics given in (28). The hybrid model block runs well-trained feedforward neural networks for the physics parameters and Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) neural networks for the residuals, utilizing states obtained from the UKF and measured inputs. All neural networks of the hybrid model are first are trained within a PyTorch environment, and then the corresponding neural networks (both feedforward and recurrent) are recreated in Matlab with the network weights and biases initialized with those trained in PyTorch.

The DRL sources its states from measured and UKF output values i.e.,
s =
{ z ,
i
r
, v , T ,
T
a m b
}
to output the proposed current magnitude. The proposed current is then passed through a CBF filter, which utilizes the Sequential Quadratic Programming (SQP) optimizer to compute the CBF filtering optimizations given in (26) and (27). Since the optimization problems are convex and only a few CBF projections are required for a framework with a well-trained DRL policy, the optimizer can perform optimization in an average time of 0.023 s (much lower than the 1-second time step), using the RL-proposed action as an initial guess. Finally, the safe current output from the CBF filter is changed to the corresponding analog voltage to control the DC power supply that charges the LiB cell.

Fig. 6 illustrates the performance of the DRL+CBF during charging across three different battery and ambient temperature scenarios. At lower temperatures, the charging current is constrained by the voltage cutoff limit imposed by the voltage CBF constraint. As temperatures increase, the maximum charging current starts to be restricted by the permissible maximum temperature, governed by the temperature and action-bound CBF constraints. The framework learns to optimize charging currents by generating profiles that effectively adhere to these imposed constraints. However, due to the minor penalties applied during CBF constraint violations, which only slightly penalize CBF projections, the DRL learns to distinguish between proposed actions and CBF-projected actions. As a result, CBF projections become minimal once the DRL is well-trained, although it continues to propose policies near the boundaries. In the evaluations presented, only two instances of small projections were observed.

Fig. 7 shows the comparison between the charging profiles generated with our framework and the manufacturer recommended CCCV profile, where the CCCV profile maintains a constant current (CC) of 0.3C (1.5 A) below temperatures of
25
∘
C
and 0.7C (3.5 A) at or above
25
∘
C
. Both profiles initially employ higher currents that decrease as charging progresses. However, it is clear from the plots that our framework significantly enhances charging speed (up to 8X at lower temperatures), unlike the highly conservative profile of the manufacturer-recommended CCCV. This underscores the fact that utilizing a learned model-based fast charging profile that adapts to age and environmental conditions can avoid the necessity of a stationary conservative profile designed to accommodate a wide range of extreme scenarios.

This paper proposes a framework to enable continually adaptive fast charging of Lithium-ion Batteries (LiBs) using current, voltage, and temperature operational measurements. This involves first modeling the complex nonlinear electrochemical and thermal dynamics of LiB with a hybrid model, where an equivalent circuit model (ECM) physics-based component is augmented by a residual neural network. A fast charging policy is then trained via DRL by exploring this virtual hybrid model through latent imagination. This approach removes the safety and operational concerns associated with exploring on actual LiB for DRL training. In addition, formal safety guarantees are introduced by filtering the proposed DRL action with control barrier function (CBF) filter including SOC, voltage, and temperature constraints. Since the non-affine temperature CBF constraint (quadratic constraint) can result in infeasible scenarios, an additional CBF constraint is introduced to ensure that the maximum temperature constraint is satisfied within the action-bound of the charger. This new constraint requires the heat generated by the diffusion current to be balanced by the heat rejection rate from the LiB near the allowable maximum temperature. By employing a reward structure that encourages rapid SOC increases while slightly penalizing CBF constraint violations, the DRL devises a policy to optimize fast charging while still allowing the DRL to effectively utilize trajectories at the boundaries of the CBF constraints.

The performance of the Hybrid model-RL-CBF framework is demonstrated using LG-M50 high-energy 21700 cylindrical cells. The Hybrid model is shown to effectively capture the evolution of terminal voltage and temperature outputs for different experiments with a wide range of SOC and temperature initialization. Using the trained hybrid model, we also demonstrated the importance of considering the action-bound while designing CBF filter for the LiB. By implementing the trained DRL policy on the LiB used to train the hybrid model, the framework demonstrated its ability to adapt policies for different initial and operational conditions while maintaining safety constraints. Moreover, the framework demonstrates significant improvements in charging speed compared to manufacturer-recommended CCCV heuristic approaches, highlighting the advantages of continual learning in avoiding conservative charging profiles.

Future work will extend the learning scheme hierarchically to support battery module and pack-level configurations. The proposed framework is mainly designed with voltage and temperature constraints to ensure operational safety, as these are key factors in controlling lithium plating and SEI-related degradation. Additionally, we plan to incorporate battery lifetime models to further enhance the framework’s ability to account for long-term degradation effects.

Habtamu Hailemichael: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Beshah Ayalew: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Clemson University’s Virtual Prototyping of Autonomy Enabled Ground Systems (VIPR-GS) , under Cooperative Agreement W56HZV-21-2-0001 with the US Army DEVCOM Ground Vehicle Systems Center (GVSC).DISTRIBUTION STATEMENT A. Approved for public release; distribution is unlimited. OPSEC9341

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: See Fig. A.8.
